required.title: [Materials] Mortar mix (Type N or Type S), bricks or concrete blocks, mixing tub or wheelbarrow, mason's trowel, jointing tool, level (4-foot), string line, line blocks
[Materials] Concrete mix (pre-mixed bags for small projects), form lumber, reinforcing wire mesh or rebar (#3 or #4 for small projects)

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: Essential Craftsman — "How to Lay Brick"

supplementary.title: [Book] Structures: Or Why Things Don't Fall Down
supplementary.author: J.E. Gordon

competence_markers.description: The student can lay brick with mortar to consistent joint width (3/8 inch), level courses, and plumb faces
The student can mix concrete to proper consistency and place it correctly in a formed area
The student understands the hydration process and can explain why concrete cures rather than dries
The student can build a self-supporting arch and explain the physics of compression that makes it work
The student understands why concrete needs steel reinforcement to handle tension
The student has built a masonry structure with at least one corner, maintaining bond pattern and quality through the turn

content.why_this_matters:
At Stretching Branches, you built with gravity and friction. Now you build with mortar and concrete — materials that transform a stack of bricks into a unified structure and a pile of gravel into a solid foundation. Mortar and concrete are chemistry in action: measured ingredients, a chemical reaction (hydration), and a result that gets stronger over time.

You will also learn the physics of compression — the force that masonry is designed to carry. Every brick wall, every stone arch, every concrete foundation works by keeping its materials in compression. Understanding compression means understanding why masonry structures built two thousand years ago are still standing, and why they will still be standing two thousand years from now.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Bricklaying** — laying brick with mortar to consistent joint width, level courses, and plumb faces
2. **Concrete mixing and placement** — understanding ratios, hydration chemistry, and proper curing
3. **Compression physics** — understanding why masonry works, what it excels at, and where it fails
4. **The arch** — building and understanding one of humanity's greatest structural inventions
5. **Reinforcement** — understanding why concrete needs steel to handle tension
6. **Foundation principles** — understanding that every structure rests on its foundation and that the foundation must be right
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Bricklaying with Mortar

Bricklaying is a discipline of rhythm and precision. Each brick is placed on a bed of mortar, pressed into position, checked for level and plumb, and the excess mortar is cut away. Each course is checked before the next begins. The rhythm — mortar, brick, check, mortar, brick, check — becomes the heartbeat of the work.

**Mortar preparation:** Mix mortar according to the manufacturer's directions. The consistency should be like thick peanut butter — stiff enough to hold its shape on the trowel but soft enough to spread. Too wet and it squeezes out under the weight of the brick. Too dry and it does not bond. Getting the consistency right is a feel that develops with practice.

**Laying technique:**
1. Spread a bed of mortar on the course below (or on the foundation for the first course) — approximately 3/4 inch thick, slightly wider than the brick
2. Butter the end of the brick to be placed — spread mortar on one end so that when it is pressed against the adjacent brick, the mortar fills the vertical joint (head joint)
3. Press the brick into the mortar bed, aligning it with the string line and checking level
4. Tap with the trowel handle to adjust position
5. Cut away excess mortar that squeezes out from the joints
6. Check level on every brick. Check plumb on every few bricks. Check string line alignment on every brick.

**Joint consistency:** The bed joints (horizontal) and head joints (vertical) should be a consistent 3/8 inch. This consistency is what gives professional brickwork its clean appearance. Joints that vary in width look amateur and create structural inconsistency.

**Tooling the joints:** After the mortar has stiffened slightly (thumbprint firm), run a jointing tool along each joint to compact the mortar surface. This improves weather resistance and appearance. The most common joint profile is a concave joint — slightly curved inward.

### Part 2: Concrete — Chemistry You Can Pour

Concrete is not just gray mud. It is a precisely proportioned mixture that undergoes a chemical reaction to become one of the strongest building materials on earth.

**Ingredients:**
- **Portland cement** — the binding agent. A powder made by heating limestone and clay to extreme temperatures and grinding the result.
- **Aggregate** — gravel and crushed stone. Provides bulk and compressive strength.
- **Sand** — fine aggregate. Fills the gaps between the coarse aggregate particles.
- **Water** — triggers the chemical reaction (hydration) that causes the cement to harden.

**The critical insight:** Concrete does not dry. It cures. The cement undergoes a chemical reaction with water (hydration) that produces crystals which bind the aggregate into a solid mass. This reaction requires water — keeping concrete moist during curing makes it stronger, not weaker. Concrete that dries out too quickly is weaker than concrete that is kept damp for the first week.

**Mix ratios matter.** Too much water makes the concrete easier to pour but weakens it — the excess water creates voids as it evaporates, and those voids are weaknesses. Too little water makes the concrete unworkable. The standard ratio for general-purpose concrete is approximately 1 part cement : 2 parts sand : 3 parts gravel, with enough water to make it workable but not soupy.

**Placement:** Pour concrete into forms (temporary wooden structures that hold the concrete in shape until it cures). Consolidate by tapping the forms or vibrating the concrete to remove air bubbles. Strike off the top surface with a straight board to create a level surface. Finish with a trowel or broom, depending on the desired texture.

### Part 3: Compression — What Masonry Does Best

Masonry is a compression material. It excels at carrying loads that push it together and fails under loads that pull it apart.

**Compression** is the squeezing force. When a brick wall carries the weight of a roof, every brick in the wall is in compression. The bricks are being squeezed between the weight above and the foundation below. Bricks and concrete are extraordinarily strong in compression — a standard concrete block can resist approximately 1,900 pounds per square inch of compressive force.

**Tension** is the pulling-apart force. Masonry is weak in tension — approximately 1/10 as strong as in compression. A brick wall that is pushed sideways (by wind or earth pressure) develops tension on one face, and that face can crack. This is why masonry walls need to be thick relative to their height and why retaining walls require special design.

**The lesson:** Every masonry structure must be designed so that the materials stay in compression. When tension is unavoidable (in beams, in walls resisting lateral force), reinforcement (steel) must be added to handle the tensile force.

### Part 4: The Arch — Geometry Conquering Gravity

Build an arch from bricks or stones and watch gravity try to pull it apart — and fail.

An arch works by converting vertical load (gravity) into diagonal forces that push outward along the curve and are resisted by the supports (abutments) on either side. Every stone in the arch is in compression — squeezed between its neighbors. As long as the geometry is right and the abutments do not move, the arch stands. No mortar is required. No fasteners. Just geometry and compression.

The arch was one of the most important structural inventions in human history. The Romans used it to build aqueducts that carried water across valleys, bridges that spanned rivers, and buildings with open interiors that flat-stone construction could not achieve. The Gothic pointed arch allowed medieval builders to create soaring cathedral interiors that still inspire awe.

**Build a small arch:** Using bricks or cut stones, build a semicircular arch on a temporary form (a curved piece of plywood or cardboard that supports the bricks during construction). Once the keystone (the top-center brick) is placed, remove the form. The arch stands on its own. Push down on the keystone. It does not fall — it pushes the force outward to the abutments. This is compression in action.

### Part 5: Reinforcement — Steel and Concrete Working Together

Concrete is strong in compression and weak in tension. Steel is strong in tension. Together, they form a composite material — reinforced concrete — that is strong in both.

**Rebar** (reinforcing bar) is embedded in concrete before it cures. When the concrete hardens around the rebar, the two materials work together: concrete handles compression, steel handles tension. A concrete beam without rebar cracks and fails under bending load (the bottom of a bending beam is in tension). The same beam with rebar at the bottom resists the tension and carries the load.

This principle — combining materials so each handles the forces it does best — is one of the most powerful ideas in engineering. You will encounter it again in every structural discipline.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Bricklaying Quality
**Developing well:** Consistent 3/8-inch joints, level courses (check with a 4-foot level), plumb faces, clean joint tooling. The wall looks like a professional laid it — or at least like someone who was taught by a professional.

**Developing slowly:** Inconsistent joint widths (some 1/4 inch, some 3/4 inch), courses that drift out of level, mortar smeared on brick faces. These are technique issues that improve with practice — have the student lay more practice sections before committing to a project wall.

### 2. Concrete Work
**Developing well:** Correct mix consistency, proper consolidation (no visible air pockets on the surface), level finish, and the student covers and moistens the concrete for proper curing without being reminded.

**Developing slowly:** Mix is too wet or too dry, the surface is uneven, the student neglects curing.

### 3. Compression Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can explain why the arch stands, why masonry walls are designed for compression, and why reinforcement is needed where tension occurs. They can point to examples in real buildings.

**Developing slowly:** The student memorizes the terms but cannot apply them. They cannot explain what is happening physically when they push on the arch keystone.
